one.en: CHILDREN – Parenting order – International relocation – Where the father is a British citizen working as a lawyer in Country D – Where the father seek…

summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – CHILDREN – Parenting order – International relocation – Where the father is a British citizen working as a lawyer in Country D – Where the father seeks the child’s relocation – Where Country D is not a signatory to the Hague Convention – Where it is in the child’s best interests to have a relationship with both parents and this can only occur if the child is living with the father – Where the parties agree that an order for sole parental decision-making in relation to major long-term issues affecting the child is appropriate – Where the father shall take all reasonable steps to register the order in the United Kingdom and/or obtain a reciprocal parenting order in the United Kingdom that can be enforced in that jurisdiction – Where the child will live with the father and there will be a three-month moratorium on time and communication with the mother

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

CAREW J:

X is nine years of age and has lived with her mother in Australia since 2019. Notwithstanding orders for her father to spend time with her, her mother has rarely complied. The father is now applying for the child to live with him in City N, in Country D (“Country D”).
For reasons that will be explained, the child will live with the father in City N, Country D and spend time with the mother during school holidays. The father will also have sole parental responsibility and sole decision-making authority for all major long-term parenting issues.

BACKGROUND

Mr Ritter (“the father”) and Ms Hermann (“the mother”) have one child together, namely, X, who was born on [redacted] 2016.
The father was born on [redacted] 1979 and is currently 46 years of age. He is a British citizen and currently resides in City N, Country D. The father is a lawyer holding a practising certificate issued in the United Kingdom (“UK”) and is a shareholder in an international law firm Firm R. He has remarried and has a two-year-old daughter and a nearly seven-month-old son. The father and his family live a very privileged life in Country D. The father’s wife is a UK citizen and has herself lived in Country D since 2012. She works part time as a personal trainer.
The mother was born on [redacted] 1981 and is currently 44 years of age. She is a dual Australian and British citizen. The mother has not re-partnered. During cross-examination, the mother asserted that she was employed during school hours by a National Disability Insurance Scheme (“NDIS”) private provider as a disability support practitioner, however she did not provide any details about the income she received. The mother receives child support of $460 per week from the father. In addition, the father pays for the child’s school fees, private health insurance, after school and vacation care costs, extracurricular activities, school uniforms and sports kit. The mother lives in a two-bedroom rental property in Brisbane, Australia.
The parents commenced a relationship in the UK in November 2015 and married on [redacted] 2018 in Country T. The father moved to City N, Country D in November 2017 to take up a position as a lawyer. The mother and the child joined the father on 22 May 2018, and the parties lived together until the mother unilaterally left Country D with the child in August 2019. The parents had separated in January 2019 but continued to live under the same roof until the mother left.
The parents divorced on [redacted] 2020.
The relationship was described by both parents to the Family Report writer, Dr O, as volatile, with escalating verbal arguments between them. The mother alleged that the father was emotionally abusive towards her, and the father alleged that throughout the relationship the mother perpetrated various forms of family violence towards him, including physical violence. Both parents denied the other’s allegations to Dr O.
Neither parent sought that the Court make a finding about historical family violence and neither parent alleged that the other parent posed a future risk to the child of family violence.
The parties have previously been involved in four separate proceedings, the first commencing on 4 October 2019, when the child was just three years of age. A final parenting order was made by consent on 31 July 2020 which provided for the parents to have equal shared parental responsibility for major long-term issues, for the child to live with the mother and to spend time with the father.
On 6 December 2021, the mother filed an Initiating Application seeking sole parental decision-making responsibility and for the child to spend supervised time with the father. The application was dismissed in April 2022.
The 2020 parenting order was varied on 31 March 2023 during contravention proceedings successfully brought by the father, for the second time, against the mother for failing to comply with the parenting order including that the child spend time with the father.
The current proceedings were commenced on 19 April 2024, when the father filed an Initiating Application seeking the child be enrolled in school following the child’s exclusion from a Brisbane private school due to serious breaches of the school’s code of conduct by the mother.
On 15 July 2025, the father filed a Further Amended Application in a Proceeding seeking time with the child between 12 September 2025 and 21 September 2025, in circumstances where the mother had not allowed him to spend time with the child since July 2024. He further sought an order allowing for future time with the child to occur both in Australia and City N, notwithstanding that Country D is not a signatory to the Hague Convention on the Civil Aspects of International Child Abduction (“Hague Convention”).
At the interim hearing conducted on 18 July 2025, counsel for the father informed the Court that the father intended to file an Amended Initiating Application seeking final orders for the child to live with him in City N, Country D and requested a trial date be set.
An ex tempore judgment was delivered varying the final parenting order dated 31 July 2020 as varied on 31 March 2023, by providing for the father to spend time with the child in Australia from 12 September 2025 until 21 September 2025. In the event the mother did not facilitate that time, it was ordered that a recovery order issue. The mother did comply with the order.
The matter was set down for a five-day hearing commencing on 15 December 2025.
Other than the most recent time in September 2025, the last time the father spent time with the child in accordance with the parenting order without issue, was between 30 April 2021 and 14 May 2021, when the child spent 14 consecutive nights with him.
The father’s estimated legal fees up until the end of what was supposed to be a five-day trial were $401,115.06.
The mother represented herself in the proceedings.
The Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”) estimated that his costs up to and including the trial would be $26,771.56.
It is important to note that while the mother represented herself, no attempt appears to have been made to file documents that comply with the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”). I really do not understand how the mother’s documents were accepted by the Registry for filing. Contrary to r 2.14, the mother’s Response document (Exhibit 6) was largely handwritten rather than typed, materially disfigured, in that pages were struck through, and the pages were not numbered. One of the mother’s affidavits was not typed in at least 12-point font size and did not have 1.5 line spacing. In neither affidavit relied upon were the paragraphs numbered. Compliance with the Rules is important to ensure that evidence relied upon or orders sought can be readily deciphered and understood. The deficiencies in the application of the Rules should be brought to the attention of the Principal Registrar.

PROPOSALS

The father proposes the child live with him in City N, Country D and that he have sole decision-making responsibility in relation to all major long-term issues for the child and that the child spend time with the mother during school holidays in both Australia and Country D. The precise terms of the order sought by the father are set out in Exhibit 11.
The mother proposes that the child continue to live with her in Australia and that she have sole decision-making responsibility in relation to all major long-term issues for the child. Doing the best I can to understand the mother’s position, and notwithstanding her repeated failure to facilitate the child spending time with the father, it seems her proposal is for the father to spend time with the child for 10 consecutive nights during two short school holiday periods and 18 consecutive nights during the Christmas school holidays. In addition, the mother proposes that there be weekly video calls between the father and the child, notwithstanding her failure to facilitate these calls for several years. The mother provided a written document purporting to set out the order proposed by her. It is Exhibit 6. The mother insists that the father’s time with the child only occur “under a mental health care plan” and/or only if the child wants to spend time with the father.
The ICL recommends that the child live with the father in City N, Country D. The ICL substantially adopts the father’s proposed order.

ISSUES

The following issues have been identified by the parties for my determination:
(1) What order would allow the child to have a relationship with both of her parents?

(2) Is it in the child’s best interests to have a relationship with both parents?

(3) Should there be an order for sole decision-making?

(4) What is the effect of Country D being a non-Hague Convention country?

(5) The effect upon the child of a change of residence to her father with a subsequent relocation to City N, Country D.

(6) To what extent, if any, does the child’s generalised anxiety disorder and signs of some traits of Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (“ADHD”) and the mother’s asserted belief that the child has autism spectrum disorder (“ASD”) have upon what order would be made in her best interests.

APPLICABLE LEGAL PRINCIPLES – PARENTING

A parenting case involving relocation is just another parenting case.[1] There are no special tests that apply. Neither parent has to establish a compelling reason for wishing to live where they want to live.[2] Nor is the enquiry one directed to whether or not a parent should be ‘permitted’ to relocate.[3] While the best interests of children remain the paramount consideration, they are not the sole consideration.[4] Where the legitimate interests of an adult conflict with the best interests of children, the former must give way.[5]
Parenting proceedings are regulated by Pt VII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”). In addition, s 43 of the Act requires the Court to have regard to several matters including:
(a) The need to protect the rights of children and to promote their welfare; and

(b) The need to ensure protection from family violence.

Every parenting decision requires the application of the relevant parts of Pt VII of the Act which sets out the objects and matters that must be considered when determining what parenting order is proper.[6]
A ‘parenting order’ is defined in s 64B of the Act and may deal with matters including:
(a) The person or persons with whom a child is to live;

(b) The time a child is to spend with another person or other persons;

(c) The allocation of parental responsibility; and

(d) The communication a child is to have with another person or persons.

The objects of Pt VII of the Act are set out in s 60B and are to ensure that the best interests of children are met, including by ensuring their safety, and to give effect to the United Nations Convention on the Rights of the Child.
In deciding whether to make a particular parenting order, the Court must regard the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration (s 60CA of the Act).
The best interests of the child are determined by reference to the matters set out in s 60CC of the Act and include: arrangements that promote the safety (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect or other harm) of the child and each person who has care of the child; any views expressed by the child; the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child; the capacity of each person who has or will have parental responsibility to provide for the child’s needs; the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child’s parents, and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so; and in considering those matters, the Court must consider any history of family violence, abuse or neglect involving the child or a person caring for the child, and any family violence order that applies or has applied to the child or a member of the child’s family.
Section 60CG of the Act imposes a statutory imperative to ensure that a parenting order does not expose a person to an unacceptable risk of family violence and empowers the Court to include in the order any safeguards that it considers necessary for the safety of those affected by the order.
Family violence is defined in s 4AB of the Act and means violent, threatening, or other behaviour by a person that coerces or controls a member of the person’s family (the family member) or causes the family member to be fearful. Examples of such behaviour include assault, sexually abusive behaviour, stalking, repeated derogatory taunts, intentional damage or destruction of property, unreasonably withholding financial support needed to meet reasonable living expenses of the family member or his or her child at a time when the family member is entirely or predominantly dependent on the person for financial support, preventing the family member from making or keeping connection with his or her family or friends, or unlawfully depriving the family member from his or her liberty, etc.
It is not necessary to make findings of fact on every factual dispute raised by the parties.[7] The paramount issue for the Court is to determine what order is in the best interests of the subject child in the particular circumstances of the case, and in the process of that determination the Court “cannot be diverted by the supposed need to arrive at a definitive conclusion”[8] on each and every factual dispute.
Each parent has parental responsibility (i.e. all the powers, responsibilities, and authority which, by law, parents have in relation to a child) for a child subject to any order made by the Court (s 61C of the Act).
Section 61D of the Act provides that a parenting order can deal with the allocation of responsibility for decision making about major long-term issues, being joint or sole decision making in relation to all or specified major long-term issues. Major long-term issues mean issues about the care, welfare, and development of the child of a long-term nature and includes issues about education, religious and cultural upbringing, health, name, and changes to living arrangements that make it significantly more difficult for the child to spend time with a parent (s 4).
Although I may not specifically discuss in these reasons each subparagraph of each relevant section of the Act, I have considered all sections as required when making my determination.[9]

WHAT ORDER WOULD ALLOW THE CHILD TO HAVE A RELATIONSHIP WITH BOTH OF HER PARENTS?

Notwithstanding a parenting order made in 2020, the mother has repeatedly contravened the order thus interfering with the child’s right to have a relationship with the father.
On 23 June 2022, the mother was found to have contravened the parenting order without reasonable excuse eight times, including when she refused to allow the child to spend time with the father between 14 December 2021 and 20 December 2021. The mother was ordered to enter into a Bond to be of good behaviour for a period of 12 months and to comply with the parenting order. Additionally, an order was made that if the mother again failed to comply with the final parenting order during the next 12 months, she would be required to pay the father’s costs and expenses fixed in the sum of $11,755.33.
The mother was required to sign the Bond by no later than 7 July 2022. The mother did not sign the Bond until 17 October 2022.
On 31 March 2023, the mother was again found to have contravened the parenting order without reasonable excuse on five further occasions, including between 4 July 2022 and 11 July 2022 when she refused to allow the child to spend time with the father. Notwithstanding the contraventions being found to fall within the category of more serious contraventions, and notwithstanding that the mother had not complied with the conditions of the earlier Bond (which had not expired at the date of the hearing), the mother was ordered to enter into another Bond to be of good behaviour for two years and to comply with the parenting order. Additionally, as a result of these further contraventions the mother was ordered to pay the sum of $11,755.33 to the father which had been suspended for 12 months conditional upon her compliance with previous court orders. The mother has not paid the father as ordered.
The mother was ordered to sign the Bond on 17 April 2023 but refused to do so. A further penalty hearing on 3 May 2023 ordered the mother to sign the Bond by 4 May 2023 and pay the father’s costs of $4,000. The mother has not paid the father as ordered.
Notwithstanding the imposition of a Bond to be of good behaviour and comply with the parenting order, the mother again contravened the parenting order by failing to facilitate the child spending time with the father from 3 April 2023 until 16 April 2023. During this period of 13 days, the father saw the child approximately three times for short durations, totalling less than two hours.
The child was to spend time with the father from 15 June 2023 until 22 June 2023. As an alternative to overnight time (which the mother objected to), the father proposed spending between four and nine hours each day with the child, to which the mother agreed in writing. Notwithstanding her agreement, the mother failed to facilitate this time, and the father did not see the child at all during his stay in Australia.
The child was to spend time with the father from 28 December 2023 until 3 January 2024. Despite the father being present in Australia, the mother failed to facilitate this time, and he did not see the child.
On 24 June 2024, a recovery order issued to deliver the child into the care of her father for a period of four weeks, being from 24 June 2024 until 22 July 2024. The child was delivered into the care of the father by the Australian Federal Police on 26 June 2024 and she remained with the father until 10 July 2024 when the mother grabbed the child from the father at a school interview.
The father’s scheduled time from 14 September 2024 until 21 September 2024 was forfeited by the father in circumstances where the mother had indicated to the Court in August 2024 that she had no intention of complying with the order.
The father was to spend time with the child from 19 December 2024 until 6 January 2025. Despite the father travelling to Australia, the mother failed to facilitate the child spending time with the father.
As noted earlier, the father spent time with the child from 12 September 2025 until 21 September 2025 pursuant to an order made on 18 July 2025.
Dr O undertook Family Report interviews on 13 November 2025, and I accept his opinion that the mother “demonstrated contempt for the significance of the child’s relationship with the father” and “did not portray the child’s relationship with the father being significant or meaningful to the child”. I formed the same impression.
During the Family Report interview, the mother proposed that the child spend no time with the father unless the child wanted to and “if and when” the child “is well (psychologically)” (emphasis in original). Again, this proposal is consistent with the mother’s position during her oral evidence, despite her apparent proposal for extended time to occur.
There are many other examples of the mother undermining the child’s relationship with the father. I will mention several.
The mother admitted saying to the father in front of the child on 3 July 2022 that he would “never get overnights” with the child, although she sought to explain her behaviour in the context of being “upset”.
The mother admitted that she did not consent to the father’s attendance at the child’s former school for Father’s Day celebrations with the child in 2022 and admitted writing to the school on 30 August 2022, saying that the child, “believes I am her mum and dad” and that she “doesn’t have a daddy”.
Further, I find that the mother said in front of the child on 8 April 2023, “He’s [Mr Ritter]. He’s not your daddy”. The mother said she did not remember making that comment. On 10 April 2023, I find that the mother said in front of the child, “He’s not your daddy ... Say goodbye to [Mr Ritter]. It’s always important to remain polite”.
The mother made the rather extraordinary claim during cross-examination that she had “done nothing in the last two years but promote [the child’s] relationship with [the father], and I have not said anything bad in front of [the father] or to [the father]”. The mother’s claim is demonstrably false.
For example, in June/July 2024 when the child was to spend four weeks with the father pursuant to an order of the court, the mother grabbed the child two weeks into the visit and failed to return her to the father. The child was only able to spend the first two weeks with the father because the police executed a recovery order to retrieve the child from the mother.
When confronted with this fact, which the mother admitted, she responded, “I’m saying about the wording, I haven’t said anything bad about [the father] ... for over two years, because I worked on myself”.
However, this claim too, is demonstrably false. On 27 June 2024, the mother said to the child during a phone call while she was spending time with the father that he will hurt her and that he is abusive. The mother denied saying this to the child, but I do not believe her. The mother’s response when challenged about her denial elicited this nonsensical retort to the father’s counsel:
... It’s your opinion. You’re not a doctor. You’re not a psychiatrist, and you’re not my psychologist, which I’ve seen for three years.
The mother’s psychologist, if she has one, was not a witness in the mother’s case and if she has been working on her attitude, she has failed.
The mother did not facilitate the child spending time with the father during the Christmas holidays in 2024 or during the June/July school holidays in 2025. A further order had to be made in July 2025 for him to be able to spend time with the child during the 2025 September/October school holidays.
The mother has also failed to use the child’s surname (which is the same as the father’s) when filling out forms, instead using her own surname for the child. When challenged about this the mother gave an entirely unconvincing explanation, suggesting that she used her surname rather than the child’s surname as a “nickname”.
I have concluded that the mother has no regard for the child’s relationship with the father and little regard for Court orders.
The mother seems to contend that despite her long history of non-compliance with Court orders, the Court should simply trust her to comply in future.
The irony is that the mother did not even comply with an order I made on 16 December 2025 that she sign a passport application for the child. The father had to bring a further application, and an order was made on 19 January 2026 permitting the issue of a passport notwithstanding the absence of the mother’s consent.
I have absolutely no reason to believe that anything will change if the child continues to live with the mother. The sad fact is that the child will not be permitted by the mother to have a relationship with the father, and the mother will continue to undermine the child’s relationship with the father.
As the trial ended after two days rather than five, the father proposed that he spend a few days with the child prior to his departure. The mother would not agree. This was a time to demonstrate to the Court that she had changed but she could not bring herself to do so.
The only prospect of the child being able to have a relationship with both parents is for her to live with the father. I accept the father’s evidence that he will promote the child’s relationship with the mother and facilitate the mother spending time with the child. Unlike the mother, the father values the child’s relationship with the mother. He has demonstrated extraordinary patience and goodwill. The father is child focussed and understands the value for the child having an ongoing relationship with the mother.

IS IT IN THE CHILD’S BEST INTERESTS TO HAVE A RELATIONSHIP WITH BOTH PARENTS?

Neither of the parents nor the ICL contended it was in the child’s best interests not to have a relationship with both parents, although for the reasons discussed, I do not believe the mother will facilitate a relationship between the father and child in the future should the child remain living with her.

SHOULD THERE BE AN ORDER FOR SOLE DECISION-MAKING?

Notwithstanding the existing parenting order for joint decision-making about major long-term issues, the mother’s failure to comply with that order has necessitated further litigation e.g. in relation to the child’s schooling.
The parents agree that an order for sole decision-making in relation to major long-term parenting issues should be made.
An order for sole decision-making is supported by the ICL.
It seems to me to be entirely appropriate for one parent to have sole parental responsibility for decisions relating to major long-term issues affecting the child.

WHAT IS THE EFFECT OF COUNTRY D BEING A NON-HAGUE CONVENTION COUNTRY?

Under the Hague Convention, there is a central process through which a parent can pursue a failure to comply with a parenting order. Country D is not a signatory to the Hague Convention which means that if the child were to live with the father in Country D and he failed to comply with the parenting order that the child spend time with the mother, she would have to attempt to enforce her parenting rights in Country D. There is no evidence about whether such proceedings are even possible let alone whether a parenting order made in this country would have any impact on any decision made by a court in Country D. Accordingly, there is a risk that the child may not have a relationship with both parents if she lives with the father in Country D and the father fails to comply with a parenting order for the child to spend time with the mother.
However, I conclude that any risk of that occurring is remote, and in coming to that conclusion I have taken into account the following:
(a) The father is a legal practitioner with a practising certificate issued in the UK;

(b) A legal practitioner must be of good character and, in particular, comply with court orders;

(c) A failure to comply with the parenting order may have significant professional consequences for him i.e. he may be struck off;

(d) During cross-examination by counsel for the ICL, the father committed to either registering the parenting order in the UK or having reciprocal orders made in the UK and I propose to order that he take all reasonable steps to honour that commitment;

(e) The father has family in the UK and visits there each year, such that proceedings might be commenced by the mother in the UK to enforce the parenting order, should that be necessary;

(f) The father impressed as a person of high moral character and I accept that he is committed to ensuring that the child continues to have a relationship with the mother;

(g) The father has at all times acted entirely appropriately throughout the recurring parenting/contravention proceedings; and

(h) Even when given the opportunity to criticise the mother, he refrained, merely saying he was disappointed by her failure to abide by the parenting order.

I considered ordering the father to pay a security bond which the mother could access in the event the father failed to comply with the parenting order, but I have decided against doing so. I accept the father’s assurance that he will comply with the parenting order and for the reasons discussed earlier I have found the risk of the father not complying with the parenting order is remote. I have also taken into account that the mother already owes the father a considerable sum pursuant to various costs orders as a result of her failure to comply with Court orders. Costs orders were made as follows:
(a) Paragraph 5 of the Order made 31 March 2023 - $11,755.33;

(b) Paragraph 1 of the Order made 14 July 2023 - $4,000;

(c) Paragraph 7 of the Order made 29 May 2024 - $12,129;

(d) Paragraph 4 of the Order made 5 August 2024 - $1,500;

(e) Paragraph 2 of the Order made 30 August 2024 - $3,000; and

(f) Paragraph 1 of the Order made 11 April 2025 - $3,954.

I made a further costs order against the mother on 19 January 2026 for $1,611.50. The total costs owed by the mother to the father pursuant to court orders are $37,949.83. However, even without this liability I would not make an order that the father pay a security bond.

THE EFFECT UPON THE CHILD OF A CHANGE OF RESIDENCE TO HER FATHER WITH A SUBSEQUENT RELOCATION TO COUNTRY D.

The trauma associated with removing the child from the mother to live with the father in Country D is undoubtedly a significant matter that must be carefully considered. The additional impact on the child related to her diagnosis of a generalised anxiety disorder and other symptoms will be separately discussed but are also relevant when considering this issue.
The Full Court in Simmons & Simmons,[10] observed that before making an order for change of residence that “was likely to cause such significant emotional distress to the children with potential lifelong implications for them” the Court has an obligation to ensure that a decision of such magnitude is based on the most comprehensive and relevant evidence available.
The child has lived all her life with the mother and since August 2019, she has lived exclusively with the mother and spent little time with the father.
I accept the father’s evidence that when the child was an infant he attended to her daily needs when at home and offered comfort and affection. The child lived in Country D until her unilateral removal by the mother in August 2019. That is now a long time ago in the child’s life; however, it appears that those early bonds have survived the father’s significant absence from her life. For example, the mother sent a message to the father on 14 May 2021, after he had spent time with the child, in which the mother stated that the child missed him very much, wanted to be with him every day, and that the child had said she loved him. More recently, during the Family Report interviews, Dr O opined that the child holds “positive feelings towards the father” noting the child’s “positive affect and demeanour” when observed with the father including the child recalling memories with the father.
Until 2021, the father spent time with the child as ordered and communicated with the child until 2023. Thereafter, his time with the child has been interrupted by the mother’s failure to comply with parenting orders. The father spent extended time with the child in Australia in June/July 2024 (15 nights) and September 2025 (nine nights). I accept the father’s evidence that the child enjoyed her time with him and was happy and contented.
However, there seems little doubt that removing the child from the mother to live with the father in Country D would be against the child’s stated wishes and “indeed [would] enact an event that the child actively fears”, according to Dr O. He opined that such an outcome “will have severe emotional and psychological impacts on the child - at least in the short term and probable long-term, which would likely include severe symptoms of grief and loss, anxiety, mood disturbance, abandonment, attachment insecurity and varied trauma responses”. In that it may “lead to potential animosity, conflict or even rejection from the child towards the father”.
Notwithstanding those dire predictions, it is important to recognise the significant caveats around the child’s stated wishes and feelings which Dr O strongly opines are highly influenced by the mother in the context of the “troubling dynamics of the child’s relationship with the mother” and “should be interpreted with high caution and circumspect[ion]”.
The child appears to have taken on a level of concern for the mother’s reaction to her having a relationship with the father that Dr O thought was very concerning. He said:
Very concerningly, the child seems to genuinely fear the prospect of allowing herself to seek a meaningful relationship with the father and develop a deeper connection – which the child may intrinsically desire – because doing so would cause the mother distress that she would feel responsible for causing, and threaten her cherished bond with the mother and indeed risk being separated from the mother altogether. The child’s fear in this regard appears evident in various contexts, including the child’s quite intense anxiety exhibited prior to and during their interviews and observations; the child’s expressed fears about being removed into the father’s care; and the child’s evident reluctance and distress about expressing their views and feelings about the father during their interview – which according to the father has also been noted by the child’s current treating psychologist.
(Emphasis in original)
Dr O opined that “there are an abundance of concerns held for the child’s wellbeing if circumstances remain unchanged including the mother’s attitudes towards the child’s relationship with the father ... and risk factors to the child being unaddressed or not mitigated against” and that “therefore it may cause the child less harm to be subjected to a change of residence with the father in [Country D]”. Dr O described the potential risk if the child remained in the mother’s sole care as being an “ongoing and severe risk of extensive harm” which he identified as “deficiencies in relation to [the mother’s] parental insight, decision-making and mental health”.
When interviewed by Dr O, he opined about the mother:
... the mother's narrative was difficult to follow or contextualise, with her views on these occasions expressed in a rather scattered or disorganised sequence - which would be described as tangential sometimes. Despite several interjections or attempts to seek clarity by the assessor, during such times the mother exhibited no insight or awareness of how her narrative was being ineffectively or confusingly portrayed and also failed to provide any clarity sought. These occasions made it very difficult at various times to make sense of the mother's narrative.
At times during her interview the mother reacted with a sense of evasiveness, defensiveness or righteousness - seemingly when she perceived that her views were being either misunderstood, challenged or negatively judged. During such occasions, the mother could become somewhat emotionally dysregulated, passive-aggressive or aggressive, or provide responses that were vague or difficult to follow logically. For example when discussing her employment and related qualifications the mother quite staunchly and intensely advocated that she was proficient in the “social sciences” with strong conviction yet provided an evasive and vague response and questioning the assessor’s personal knowledge.
(Emphasis in original)
The mother’s presentation during the trial was consistent with the observations made and opinions expressed by Dr O.
The mother described to Dr O “a very limited support network” comprising only her mother, with whom she has had a troubled relationship, and one close friend whom the mother described as, “we (child and herself) have one true friend” (emphasis in original).
Dr O expressed concern about some of the views and beliefs the mother expressed about the “child’s symptoms, emotional and medical health which are disputed by the father – and are also inconsistent with information provided by others who are directly involved with the child (school staff, paediatrician, etc)”.
Throughout her oral evidence the mother was insistent that the child was autistic notwithstanding her concession that the child has not been independently diagnosed with autism. The mother insisted that the child “has the symptoms”. She accused the father of not allowing her “to get an NDIS plan for my autistic daughter”.
The mother did not dispute that she has referred to the child as “disabled” stating when challenged, “[a]utism is technically a medical disability”. The mother referred to the child as a “special needs” child who required a personalised learning plan which the mother said had now been implemented.
The mother presented as extremely keen to have the child diagnosed with autism level 2. The mother conceded that a paediatrician, Dr U, said that the mother had “pressed” her for an autism diagnosis. The mother conceded that she had also asked Dr H to diagnose the child with autism, but he declined. The mother further conceded that she had asked a psychologist, Ms V, to assess the child for autism and that she refused because she did not have the father’s consent. The mother conceded that she had nevertheless presented the child to Autism Queensland for assessment and that the child was assessed with autism level 1 which they later retracted. The mother conceded that she would prefer to have the child assessed with autism level 2 so that she can obtain NDIS funding.
The mother conceded saying to the father’s solicitor 12 months ago that the child “is now autistic, and she needs to be on NDIS, specialist 24-hour care and see a clinical psychologist immediately”.
The mother denied having stated during an intake session with a psychologist, Ms W, on 4 July 2024, that the child “would meet the old criteria of a borderline schizophrenic” diagnosis. The statement was in quotation marks attributed to the mother. The mother said the psychologist had made a mistake, but it seems unlikely that a psychologist would make such an error.
The mother conceded emailing the child’s current school on 10 November 2025, “regarding the possible transfer of school as [the child] doesn’t feel it’s a collaboration as such”. The mother said she could move to the Sunshine Coast, or to northern New South Wales, or to Paris, stating, “I talk a lot” and “I don’t like Brisbane City and the people. So I tell a lot of people that Paris obviously has – I follow markets and a holistic view which is Paris. ... That’s how I talk” and “they’re all just ideas”.
During her interview with Dr O, the mother was “very keen to portray ... that the child was very dependent upon her” and “referred to the child and herself as “we” numerous times during her interview” (emphasis in original). The mother “openly and repeatedly portrayed that the father’s relationship with the child was insignificant, meaningless and was not sought by the child”. For example, saying, “He’s not the father ... we had an affair, he had a baby, he divorced me and abandoned us in Australia” (emphasis in original). During the trial, the mother accepted that there may well be something in Dr O’s opinion that her relationship with the child may be enmeshed, conceding “Yes, there’s a problem with the attachment, I agree. She’s too attached to me and that’s why I want a psychologist”.
The mother referred to the child’s “significant emotional and mental health needs” on numerous occasions during her interview with Dr O. The mother also did so during the trial. At times, the mother referred to the child as “disabled” and gave the impression that the child was significantly “unwell”. As with Dr O, the mother failed to provide specific clarity about such issues. Instead, “the mother’s descriptions of the child’s varied issues were rather generalised and catastrophised”. The mother repeatedly “externalised blame about how the child’s needs have not been adequately met, including school staff, professionals and the father”. The mother seemed fixated on obtaining a mental health care plan for the child and confirming her own “diagnosis” that the child is autistic. At one stage during her interview with Dr O, the mother said – “We are living with PTSD on the autism spectrum” (emphasis in original). There is no evidence that either the mother or the child has been diagnosed with PTSD or autism. The mother told Dr O that she has been “challenged by persistent “generalised” anxiety, and “PTSD” from the “whole family court stuff”” (as per original). Indeed, the mother said she was unable to stand (rather than sit) at the Bar table during the trial because of her anxiety and when asked why she did not use paragraph numbers in her affidavits she said, “I just find them [the affidavits] so stressful, I get the severe panic attacks when I’m doing them” and that her panic attacks are “really bad”.
The mother apparently consults a “counsellor”, Ms S, but she was not a witness in the trial. Although Ms S was not a witness, some of her notes form part of Exhibit 8 and the content is at times alarming and supports, to some extent, the concerns expressed about the mother by Dr O. For example, her notes of an appointment with the mother on 30 September 2025 include the following:
- Reports experiencing severe emotional dysregulation for six months ...
...
- Describes chronic fatigue symptoms for six months with whole body feeling like it’s slowly dying

...
- Episodes involve anger, frustration, sadness, and annoyance ...
- Social history: Single mother to nine-year-old daughter with autism and ADHD, works part-time in 2-4 hour shifts as NDIS support worker, no social life or friends ...
In relation to the father, Dr O opined that the father was:
... heavily child focussed and he impressed as being very fond and caring towards the child. He demonstrated sound parental insight and exhibited diligent consideration of the child’s developmental and emotional needs. In general, the father’s parental insight, skill and capacity impressed as being highly developed and quite sophisticated.
... [the father] appeared to identify the value and significance of the mother’s relationship with the child and that he fundamentally supported this ...
A major reason for the father pursuing a change in the child’s primary care is due to his ever-increasing concerns about the mother’s propensity to accuse “everyone of abusing [the child] ... because they just don’t follow her view” (emphasis in original). This propensity will be discussed later in these Reasons. The father viewed himself as able to provide the child with the stability and empathy that was lacking in her life with the mother. That said, the father made it plain to Dr O that “[i]f [the mother] was allowing [him] in [the child’s] life and providing her with stability – this proposal would never have happened” (emphasis in original). The father saw his proposal as “the only way” (emphasis in original) for “both parents to be reliably involved and share a relationship with the child”. Dr O was impressed that the father had “duly considered how significantly impactful and challenging such a drastic change may be for [the child]”. I accept Dr O’s opinions and observations.
I am satisfied that the father has not underestimated the potential difficulties he may face if the child lives with him. The father has sourced and consulted appropriate professionals about considerations and strategies to assist him and the child and who can support the child in what is anticipated to be a traumatic transition. The father has identified an appropriate school for the child in Country D. I am also satisfied that the father has the support of his wife and his mother, both of whom impressed as loving and caring women.

TO WHAT EXTENT, IF ANY, DOES THE CHILD’S GENERALISED ANXIETY DISORDER AND SIGNS OF SOME TRAITS OF ADHD AND THE MOTHER’S ASSERTED BELIEF THAT THE CHILD HAS ASD HAVE UPON WHAT ORDER WOULD BE MADE IN HER BEST INTERESTS.

The mother asserts that the child is “neurodivergent”, “autistic” and has “special needs” and that the father has been “blocking care for [the child]”.
The child recently attended upon a clinical psychologist, Dr Y, for six sessions. In a letter dated 8 December 2025 to the child’s treating paediatrician, Dr H, Dr Y noted that the child was “a creative and high energy child who has been a delight to work with” (Exhibit 12). The mother had reported to Dr Y that the child had “significant emotional outbursts at home related to triggers such as changes in routine or delays”. Dr Y noted that she had not observed this level of emotionality nor had the father during his two-week visit with the child in September 2025. Dr Y opined that as children tend to show their emotions more to the primary carer, this inconsistency was not unusual.
Dr Y described the child’s strengths including:
(a) Her strong attachment to the mother and to her cat;

(b) Her reported enjoyment of school and extracurricular activities;

(c) Her engagement and happiness in every session;

(d) That any disappointment in the school context was short lived;

(e) Her strong (recent) friendship with a child at school;

(f) That she was generally very chatty during sessions;

(g) That she was reward motivated;

(h) That she responded well to concepts such as ‘noticing the positives’ and gratitude;

(i) That she was very creative; and

(j) That writing is a passion.

Dr Y’s main impressions included:
(a) That she did not observe significant anxiety nor did the child report unusual levels of anxiety;

(b) That any discussion about the father or her time with the father was observed to cause the child sadness and confusion. The child’s narrative about the father is “one of abandonment”;

(c) That while distractibility/restlessness was observed it improved with behavioural strategies and adjustments e.g. increased breaks, reward and pace;

(d) That the child displays poor eye contact at times;

(e) Some tics were noted including repetitive touching of body parts; and

(f) The child sometimes struggled to label her feelings.

It is of note that Dr Y expressed concern “about social isolation and disconnection from other students” and that the child’s narrative about the father is “one of abandonment”. There is no evidence that the father has ever abandoned the child. Indeed, he has gone to extraordinary lengths to maintain his relationship with the child despite constant interference by the mother.
The child’s year 3, semester 2 report card indicates achievements i.e. achieving above the expected year level, in English, humanities and social sciences, dance, music, and visual arts. The child performed at the expected year level in religion, mathematics, science, media arts, technologies, health and physical education and Italian (Exhibit 13). Her effort across all subjects has been consistent. The report card describes teaching the child as “a pleasure”. There is no indication that the child has had emotional outbursts or that she has presented with any concerning behaviours. It was noted that the child had formed a strong friendship in semester 2 with one child.
Dr H is the child’s paediatrician although he has only seen her on three occasions, 17 October 2023, 11 March 2024, and 21 July 2025. In a letter from Dr H dated 25 November 2025, he confirms his diagnosis of generalised anxiety disorder for the child and that she “also presents with symptoms suggestive of ADHD” (Exhibit 5). Dr H recommended psychological intervention to help the child develop skills for emotional regulation and to manage her anxiety. He noted that it had taken some time for the parents to agree on a psychologist for the child to see. Dr H opined that “[f]or a child with anxiety, consistency at home and school, along with opportunities to build strong and secure connection with caregivers, is vital”. I note that when specifically asked by the mother during a consultation on 17 October 2023 whether the child might have ASD, Dr H reassured that on her clinical presentation that seemed less likely. Dr H did not change his view when he saw the child on 11 March 2024 or in 2025.
Prior to seeing Dr H, the child saw a different paediatrician, Dr U. Dr U had a Zoom consultation with the mother on 24 February 2023 regarding the child’s Conners Comprehensive Behaviour Rating Scale result. In her consultation record she notes the inconsistent scoring of the child’s symptoms between the mother and the child’s teacher. Further, in the consultation record dated 23 March 2023, Dr U, noted in relation to the child, that “it will be hard to differentiate [between] Anxiety and ADHD sympt[om]s”. Dr U further noted that (in relation to the child) the mother had “scored high for all areas – Autism, conductive disorder and oppositional disorder”. Dr U did not agree that the child suffered from those conditions. Further assessment based on further input from the school and the mother was to be undertaken six months later but the mother did not make any further appointments.
As earlier noted, the father raises concerns about the mother’s reactions to people who do not adopt her narrative about the child. For example, the mother’s behaviour at the child’s private school, Z School, resulted in the child being excluded from the school in 2023. The mother’s behaviour towards the principal, teachers, and other staff at the school was appalling and described variously by the principal as rude, derogatory, defamatory, disrespectful, insulting, and in breach of the school’s Parent Code of Conduct. The mother levelled a litany of accusations against various members of the school including the following:
(a) “You have abused us”;

(b) Two named teachers were accused of “lying over and over again” alleging “false behaviours done by [the child] at school”;

(c) Two named teachers engaged in “using adult manipulation, plotting, sabotage”;

(d) Two named teachers were accused of having “an army” and “bullying”;

(e) “... you and the managers of the school are a pack of predators hunting in the day for food when it’s right in front of them”;

(f) “[The child] and I believe we have been abused by your staff, school now, previously we were unhappily bullied and attacked by your staff even having them implying my observations and viewings were of no substance nor merit”.

On one occasion, the police were called to remove the mother from the after-school hours care program because of her behaviour.
As bad as the mother’s behaviour was to the school, her failure to prepare the child for the exclusion from the school demonstrated an egregious disregard for the child’s feelings. The child attended her last day at the school without having been told it was to be her last day. When it became apparent that the child had no idea, the deputy principal was left with the unenviable task of informing the child, who was understandably distraught. The mother told Dr O that the child’s reaction was, “[a]bsolutely horrific” and that she “cried and was hysterical about it ... it was traumatic for her ... she felt safe there” (emphasis in original). The mother failed to accept any responsibility for the child’s distress or the circumstances of her exclusion from the school.
The mother showed no embarrassment or remorse for her behaviour that had such serious consequences for the child.
In an email circulated at the child’s previous school, it was stated that the mother “comes across extremely rude, insulting and condescending to me and the staff. We are unable to have a rational conversation with [the mother] due to her fabricating all interactions to fit her narrative” (emphasis in original). When asked about this email the mother said, “I complained about them, and they complained about me”.
The mother also made the bizarre claim that her neighbours had searched her bins for evidence against her. In a report to police on 12 February 2024, the mother reported “[the father] has convinced the school to arrange people to check her bins”. During cross-examination, the mother doubled down on this claim, suggesting though that “[t]he school organised it, because I tried to sue the school”.
The mother said she had “tried to sue” the child’s current school and K Education “for [the child’s] special needs”.
When it was put to the mother that she had, throughout the proceedings, threatened the father, his solicitors, the court, the child’s school and teachers to obtain her desired outcome, the mother responded, “Not threatened. I’ve just told people about my thoughts and feelings, and they don’t like it”. This latter comment about the mother just stating her “thoughts and feelings” was a common refrain throughout her oral evidence. I reject the mother’s description of her behaviour. The mother repeatedly claimed that she had apologised for her behaviour but there is no evidence that the mother has ever apologised to the many persons she has abused.
I share the opinion expressed by Dr O about the “abundance of concerns held for the child’s wellbeing if circumstances remain unchanged” and that “it may cause the child less harm to be subjected to a change of residence with the father” in Country D. The mother’s behaviour towards those who do not readily adopt her view that the child is disabled, autistic, neurodivergent, and in need of an NDIS plan, is troubling to say the least. The mother impressed as fixated on achieving a diagnosis for the child which accords with her own views, the symptoms for which are largely inconsistent with observations made by teachers, doctors, and the child’s current psychologist.
I accept Dr O’s opinion that should the child remain living with the mother there is a potential risk of an “ongoing and severe risk of extensive harm” due to the mother’s “deficiencies in relation to her parental insight, decision-making and mental health”. There is no evidence that the mother has addressed the many symptoms she complained about to her counsellor, Ms S, as recently as September 2025. The mother has very limited family or other support and certainly does not seem committed to maintaining the child’s current place of residence or school. The mother has expressed intentions to remove the child from her current school and move with the child to various possible places including overseas.

WHAT PARENTING ORDER IS IN THE CHILD’S BEST INTERESTS?

There is no prospect of the child being able to have a relationship with the father if she remains living with the mother. The mother has no insight into the impact of her behaviour and decisions on the child. For example, the child was excluded from a school she loved because of the mother’s abusive behaviour. Some of the mother’s ideas are bizarre to say the least. For example, that the child’s school had organised for neighbours to search her bins for evidence against her. The mother has undermined and denigrated the father to and in the presence of the child. For example, the mother told the child that the father will hurt her. Such an untruth is egregious.
The child has been deprived of her loving father for too long and there is no prospect of that changing unless there is a change in the child’s residence. I propose to do so notwithstanding that it will mean the child will live with the father in a country that is not a signatory to the Hague Convention.
In the order I propose to make, the child will live with the father in Country D where he currently lives and works. It is conceivable that he may at some point return to live in the UK with his wife and family (or some other country given his international legal experience). Accordingly, I consider it sensible to make an order which as far as possible limits the prospect of the parents having to return to Court if the father’s place of residence changes. It is also likely that the father will continue to visit the UK from time to time, while living elsewhere, and in such circumstances, it is conceivable that the child’s time with the mother may occur in the UK. The father will have sole decision-making in relation to all major long-term issues. I have also acceded to the father’s proposed order in relation to restraints against both parents denigrating the other parent not because there is any evidence of the father denigrating the mother but because he was intent on the order appearing to be “fair” in the mother’s eyes. As the father will have sole decision-making power in relation to major long-term issues (which includes what school the child attends) I see no need to make the order proposed by him “permitting” him to enrol the child in school. He will have this power by reason of the sole decision-making order.
All parties had the opportunity to make submissions about the orders proposed by the other parties. I have largely adopted the father’s proposed parenting order. To the extent that I have diverged from the father’s proposed order, I have done so to achieve a parenting order that is, in my view, in the child’s best interests and least likely to lead to further parenting proceedings.
In relation to travel documents for the child, an order was made on 19 January 2026 pursuant to s 11(1)(b)(i) of the Australian Passports Act 2005 (Cth) permitting the child to have an Australian travel document as defined by s 6(1) of that Act, notwithstanding the absence of consent by the mother. While the father sought to discharge all previous Orders, it is apparent that this should not extend to paragraph 1 of the order made on 19 January 2026 nor to the various costs orders made against the mother.
